metric_value: 'I see a rapid adoption reaching mainstream in Q2 of next year. These should ramp up very rapidly because Intel has priced them to drive market acceptance.' — Peter Kastner, Aberdeen Group
'Quad-core becomes really exciting for the many IT organizations that are looking at server consolidation through virtualization' — Kastner on enterprise server driver
'On a server, which can use multiple cores, almost by nature this should be very attractive for a huge percentage of volume server shipments' — Kastner on quad-core server TAM
Kastner described quad-core performance in gaming, multimedia, and high-end consumer apps as 'awesome.' 'All the reviews I've read have said this is your Christmas dream'
notes: Consumer side framing — Core 2 Quad Christmas 2006 / Q1 2007 uptake

metric_value: Xeon 5300 up to 50% faster than dual-core Xeon 5100; Core 2 Extreme up to 80% faster for highly threaded apps
'In one year, we've achieved a four-fold increase in performance. That is stunning.' — Pat Gelsinger, Intel (via Financial Times)
Dell finally launched Opteron-based servers in 2006 despite years of Intel-exclusivity — triggering Intel's quad-core urgency
notes: Context: Intel responding to Dell/AMD alignment

metric_value: Core 2 Quad Q6600 launched Jan 2007 at $851, dropped to $266 by July 2007, driving mainstream quad-core adoption in Q2-Q3 2007 exactly as Kastner predicted
By 2012 server virtualization had become the dominant enterprise-server architecture (VMware + Hyper-V + KVM), exactly as Kastner framed it as quad-core's killer app in 2006
